exemplar.title: The relationship between the frequency of sound waves in a tube and its amplitude

detailedExemplarData.title: The relationship between the frequency of sound waves in a tube and its amplitude

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data are communicated clearly and precisely: raw and processed tables are complete, units and significant figures are largely consistent, and graphs are well labeled.
Theoretical harmonic frequencies are presented with uncertainties and error bars, showing solid data-processing skills.
Sample calculations and multi-trial summaries support transparency in data handling.
weaknesses: Uncertainty analysis omits microphone dB uncertainty and oversimplifies propagation (additive rather than fractional).
Data-processing inaccuracies are evident (large Â±27 Hz on first harmonic; uniform Â±1 Hz uncertainty without proper standardâdeviation calculation).
Raw-data tables are cluttered with merged cells and inconsistent formatting, reducing readability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of results clearly meets the 5â6 band, but both uncertainty consideration and data-processing have significant omissions (band 3â4). The balanced strengths and omissions support an overall score of 4 in Data Analysis.

strengths: The research question is precise and embedded in a well-defined acoustic context, linking standing waves in closed tubes to musical-instrument applications.
The methodology is described in 15 clear, reproducible steps with specified equipment models, measurement resolutions, trial numbers and risk assessment.
Preliminary trials and justifications (e.g. moving from phone speaker to analog generator) demonstrate thoughtful method refinement.
weaknesses: Minor omissions in specifying Audacityâs spectral settings and sweep-function implementation leave small reproducibility gaps.
Explanations of some control measures (e.g. environmental noise, ambient conditions) are brief rather than deeply explained.
A few minor spelling and labeling inconsistencies (e.g. âamplitudeâ vs âsound-pressure levelâ) distract from clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research Design strands place work mainly in the 5â6 band for question context and procedure, with methodological considerations in the 3â4 band. The overall qualityâspecific context, highly reproducible method, and thoughtful planningâjustifies a score of 5, reflecting strong performance with only minor omissions.

strengths: The conclusion restates key findings with numerical gradients, R-values and percentage errors, maintaining consistency with the analysis.
Comparison to accepted scientific context (classical resonance theory and relevant literature) is relevant and partly quantitative, discussing end-correction and amplitude discrepancies.
weaknesses: Explicit discussion of how measurement uncertainties affect the key findings is missing.
Deeper quantitative linkage between experimental error margins and conclusions would strengthen justification.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strands align with the 3â4 band: the conclusion is consistent and supported by data, and the scientific-context comparison is relevant but not fully developed. A score of 4 reflects solid performance with room for deeper uncertainty integration.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses are identified (microphone range, end-correction, speaker-distance effects) and their qualitative impacts are described.
Suggested improvements (wider-range microphone, apply end-correction, extend frequency range) are realistic and directly tied to the weaknesses.
weaknesses: The relative impact or magnitude of each methodological limitation is not quantitatively explained.
Improvement suggestions lack prioritization or quantitative justification, remaining at a descriptive level.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation strands occupy the 3â4 band: weaknesses and improvements are specific and relevant but described rather than fully explained. The work merits a score of 3 for identifying targeted issues and realistic remedies without in-depth impact analysis.

strandSelections.reasoning: **The research question is precise (âHow does varying the frequency â¦ affect amplitude inside a closed-end tube?â) and the student embeds it in a clearly defined acoustic context (standing waves, odd-harmonic resonance, musical-instrument relevance).** The background theory, equations and diagram demonstrate exactly why a closed tube is an appropriate system, so the reader immediately understands the scope and significance of the study. This exceeds a broad mention and meets the descriptor of a **specific and appropriate context**, meriting the 5â6 band. Minor weaknesses (e.g. minor spelling errors, a brief title that mentions âamplitudeâ rather than âsound-pressure levelâ) do not detract materially from the contextualisation, so a mid-band 5 is appropriate.

Instrument resolutions are quoted and propagated for temperature and harmonic frequencies; average-range method is used for uncertainty in means and error bars are plotted. **However, the microphone dB uncertainty is ignored, amplitude errors are absent, and propagation is oversimplified (e.g. additive not fractional).** Consequently uncertainties are considered but with significant omissions, matching the 3-4 band; a mid-band 3 reflects the partial treatment.

The student **identifies independent, dependent, controlled and uncontrolled variables in detailed tables and justifies why they matter.** Instrument uncertainties are listed and some control measures (fixed speaker distance, constant amplitude) are described. However, explanations of *why* each control reduces error are brief, and discussion of sampling rate, microphone calibration or environmental noise is missing. The considerations are therefore **described rather than fully explained**, corresponding to the top of the 3-4 band; 4 is awarded.

Tables of systematic and random errors list specific weaknesses (microphone range, end correction, speaker distance) and outline their impact qualitatively, but **the relative magnitude or direction of each effect is not analysed in depth.** Hence weaknesses are *described* rather than fully explained, placing the strand solidly in the 3-4 band with a score of 3.

**The procedure is laid out step-by-step (15 enumerated steps), instrument models and measurement resolutions are quoted, and distances, temperature checks and repetition strategy (eight trials) are all specified.** A reader could assemble the same equipment and follow the instructions with little ambiguity; even preliminary trials and risk assessment are recorded. The only notable omissions are how Audacityâs spectral settings were configured and how the 5 Hz sâ»Â¹ sweep is achieved, but these are minor. Hence the description allows reproduction, fulfilling the 5â6 band; a conservative 5 recognises the small gaps.

Averages, percentage errors and linear fits are calculated correctly, but **propagation of combined uncertainties is rudimentary and the very large Â±27 Hz on the first harmonic indicates inconsistent data processing.** The student acknowledges microphone response yet does not adjust data accordingly. Thus processing is *mostly appropriate* but contains notable inaccuracies, situating the work in the 3-4 band; 3 is awarded.

For each listed weakness, the student proposes a realistic remedy (wider-range microphone, apply end correction, extend frequency range, control speaker distance). **Improvements are relevant and feasible but lack quantitative justification or prioritisation**, so they are *described* rather than *explained*. This again corresponds to a middle 3-4 band level; 3 is assigned.

**Raw-data tables (complete appendix), processed tables, sample calculations and multi-trial graphs are all included and correctly labelled.** Units are given consistently and significant figures are largely respected, so communication is generally both clear and precise. Minor layout clutter (merged cells, inconsistent commas as decimals) prevents a perfect score, so a 5 within the top band is justified.

The student compares results with classical resonance theory and with a journal article, discussing end-correction and microphone response. **This comparison is relevant and partly quantitative (average error 24 % vs 5 % in literature).** The explanation of discrepancies is plausible but not deeply developed, so the work fits the âsome relevant comparisonâ descriptor near the top of the 3-4 band.

The conclusion restates major findings, cites numerical gradients, R-values and percentage errors, and ties them to the research question. **It is broadly consistent with the analysed data** and recognises deviations (experimental frequencies lower than theoretical). Nonetheless, reliance on partially flawed processing and the absence of explicit uncertainty comparison weaken the justification, so it reaches the 3-4 bandâs upper end.

annotations.comment: The introduction effectively situates the research within acoustic applications by explaining standing waves in closed tubes and relevance to instrument design.
The research question is precise and clearly framed (âHow does varying the frequency (100â1200 Hz)â¦?â), providing a strong foundation for the investigation.
The background defines amplitude as displacement but then measures it in dB; clarify that dB represents sound-pressure level rather than true amplitude.
The variables table describes amplitude measurement via phone mic but omits calibration of frequency response; include mic response curve or calibration procedure.
Not measuring ambient pressure and humidity may introduce speed-of-sound variation; consider logging these parameters for accurate v(T,P,humidity).
The method outlines a 5 Hz/s sweep but does not describe how the generator achieves this rate; specify the sweep function settings or manual stepping procedure.
Preliminary trials are well justified: phone speaker limitations and adoption of analog generator and oscilloscope show thoughtful method refinement.

Uncertainty in mean temperature is calculated via range/2 but not propagated fractionally into v; adopt full propagation $$
\Delta c = 0.61\,\Delta T$$.
Presenting theoretical harmonic frequencies with uncertainties demonstrates clear and precise data processing.
The raw-data extract table is densely formatted with merged cells, which reduces clarity; reorganize columns or separate runs for precision.
The discussion of microphone frequency response on the trial-1 amplitude graph is apt, but quantitative correction (mic sensitivity curve) would improve accuracy.
Extracting peak frequencies in Table 8 is clear, but the Â±1 Hz uncertainty appears uniform; consider using standard deviation of repeated peaks.
In Table 9 you label the second column as âStandard deviationâ but calculate range/2; apply correct formula for standard deviation from your data set.
The linear-fit analysis cites R-values but omits uncertainties in slope/intercept; include error bars on fit parameters for a quantitative comparison.
The experimental-error formula is correct, but clarify that âexpected valueâ refers to the theoretical harmonic frequency to avoid ambiguity.
The conclusion effectively restates the research question, references R-values, and compares to literature, showing strong consistency with analysis.
The conclusion omits explicit discussion of how measurement uncertainties impact the key findings; include a statement on uncertainty influence.
Identification of systematic errors (microphone range, end correction, frequency range) is specific and ties directly to experimental impact.
Suggested improvements are relevant (wider-range mic, account for end correction, extend harmonics) but lack prioritization or quantitative justification.
Random-error table correctly identifies speaker distance issue; consider adding multiple distance measurements to quantify variation.
Strengths table appropriately highlights literature comparison and standardized protocol, reinforcing methodological rigor.

rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
rubricData.name: Physics IA

similarExemplars.title:
How does the length of the pipe affect the natural frequency of the air column in the pipe at constant air temperature when the pipe is:
a)	open at both ends and air is blown close to one end?
b)	open at one and closed at the other end, and air is blown close to the open end?
Natural frequency is the frequency that has the highest intensity and is the loudest of all frequencies which are produced with air blowing.

userSubmission.researchQuestion:
How does the length of the pipe affect the natural frequency of the air column in the pipe at constant air temperature when the pipe is:
a)	open at both ends and air is blown close to one end?
b)	open at one and closed at the other end, and air is blown close to the open end?
Natural frequency is the frequency that has the highest intensity and is the loudest of all frequencies which are produced with air blowing.
